publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2020-04-21 23:46:53 -0400.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.physdesc: Journals, correspondence, manuscripts, photographs, chapbooks, typescript, memoranda, and paintings.
did.abstract: Primarily incoming correspondence from students and teaching colleagues; fellow literary figures; and family members. The letters frequently enclose poetry for review; snapshots of family members; and photos of Ammons and other literary figures. The papers provide sources for the study of modern American poetry, including the publication of poetry in various journals. Some letters discuss the publication of Ammons' own books of poetry; others highlight activities of small presses. Letters and records of writers' conferences underscore Ammons' role in Southern regional literature. Also, material detailing his role in Cornell's Department of English and its creative writing program.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Poet and university professor Archie Randolph Ammons was born near Whiteville, N.C., in 1926. He earned a reputation as one of the nation's leading poets in the decades after he joined the Cornell University faculty in 1963, becoming Goldwin Smith Professor of Poetry a decade later. Recipient of the Bollingen Prize and the National Book Award and Critics Circle Award for poetry, Ammons was one of the first recipients of a MacArthur Foundation fellowship in 1981.

arrangement.head: SERIES LIST
arrangement.p: 3. Combined Published and Unpublished or UnknownBox 122-124
Series VII. Broadcasts, Recordings, and Readings Boxes 124, 126, 128, 132, C-14-15, C-18
Series X. Writings and Works by Others Boxes 90-92, 116
Series XI. Photographs, Memorabilia, and Artwork Boxes 93-97, 115-117, 124, 129, 131, map case folder 1
2. Works about A. R. Ammons (criticism, reviews, interviews, Ph.D. Thesis, posthumous compilations)Boxes 98-99, 106-108
Series XIII. Phyllis Ammons' Files Boxes 98, 116, 127, 129-130

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Archie Ammons papers, #14-12-2665. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Primarily incoming correspondence from students and teaching colleagues; fellow poets and other literary figures; and a few family members. The letters frequently enclose chapbooks and typescript or manuscript poetry for review; snapshots of family members; and formal and informal photographs of Ammons and other literary figures. Envelopes frequently bear Ammons's draft replies to letters received as well as draft poetry, lecture notes, and other matter. The papers provide sources for the study of modern American poetry, including the publication of poetry in journals such as The Hudson Review, The Manhattan Review, and The Bluefish; and in such Southern literary journals as The Pembroke Magazine and The North Carolina Literary Review. Letters from W. W. Norton & Company discuss the publication of Ammons' own books of poetry, while others highlight activities of such small presses as Palaemon Press Limited and the Jackpine Press. Letters and records of writers conferences sponsored at the Reynolds Homestead Learning Center in Critz, Va., underscore Ammons' role in Southern regional literature. Letters, departmental memoranda, and "Recommendations" files detail his role in Cornell's Department of English and its creative writing program. And letters from 1981 and later record his relationship with the MacArthur Foundation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Also included are sixty-eight journals, in which Ammons interfiled journal entries, poems, and correspondence. They detail Ammons' growth as a poet, and his relationship with his mentor Josephine Miles and editor John Benedict. Literary manuscripts include published and unpublished poetry. Audio and video tapes, including tapes of Ammons' reading his own poetry and "Poetry for A.R. Ammons," a videotape from Ammonsfest, Cornell University, April 3, 1998, a celebration of A.R. Ammons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Journal #1, Journal (letters to Phyllis) 1947-1949, photographs: Archie and son John, Archie and grandson, Archie.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Correspondence, 1997-1998; Over 50 watercolor paintings dating from the late 1970s to the late 1980s.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: Major correspondents include John G. Benedict, James Bertolino, Philip E. Booth, Frederick Buell, David Burak, James Burgess, Kathryn Stripling Byer, Stephen Cushman, Ephim Fogel, Kenneth Frazelle, Philip H. Fried, Alice Fulton, William Harmon, David Ardene Hinton, Josephine Jacobsen, Phyllis Janowitz, Cathrael Kazin, Milton Kessler, David Lehman, Gay Leonhardt, Michael McFee, Jim Merod, Frederick Morgan, Reeve Parker, Robert Dale Schultz, John E. Sitter, Mark Soifer, Stephen Tapscott, Helen Hennessy Vendler, Hugh Walthall, Jean West, Emily Herring Wilson, and Stuart Wright.

accessrestrict.head: Access Restrictions:
accessrestrict.p: Access restricted to the permission of the curator.

accessrestrict.head: Access Restrictions:
accessrestrict.p: Access to Box 130 restricted until 2033 or by permission of John Ammons.

userestrict.head: Restrictions on Use:
userestrict.p: For that part of the collection that is available for research, researchers may view one folder at a time.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Due to the fragility and potential degradation of moving image and sound recordings, viewing and listening is limited to items that have been digitized. If an item is in another media format, you may request to have the item digitized for access. Information on ordering access copies may be found on the Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections webpage.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Audio visual media that has been digitized: V-410

"Bridge," "Gravelly Run," "Visit," and "Corson's Inlet" The Contemporary American Poets pp.1-8
"Dunes" and "Corson's Inlet" Possibilities of Poetry: An Anthology of Contemporaries pp.319-323

"Standing Light Up," "Looking Way Off," "Somers Point,"First Cold," and "White Echo" Poems for a Small Planet: Contemporary American Nature Poetry pp.9-12

did.unittitle: "Coon Song" Poetry to Read Out Loud p. 169

Albright, Alex, "Ponds and Mudbanks, and Ditchbanks, Brierberries, Things of That Kind: A Conversation with A.R. Ammons," with excerpts from "Tape for the End of the Year," and the poem "Broad Brush" North Carolina Literary Review Vol. 1 No. 1 (Summer 1992) pp. 7, 46.
"Alligator Holes Down Along About Old Dock," North Carolina Literary Review," vol. I, no. 2 (Spring 1993) p. 228.
"Antithesis," "Range," and "Sweetened Change," The Hampden-Syndney Poetry Review (Winter 1979) pp. 1-3.
"A.R. Ammons: Interview and Poems," with "The Fairly High Assimilation Rag," and "The Spiral Rag," The Manhattan Review vol. 1, no. 2 (Fall 1980) pp. 1-34.
"The Brook Has Worked Out the Prominence of a Bend," and "Strolls," Poetry, vol. CXXXIII, no. 1 (November 1978) pp. 80-81.
"By the Boulder Cluster the Wind," The Hudson Review, vol. XXX, no. 3 (Autumn 1977) pp. 371.
"Chiseled Clouds," broadside included in commemorative brochure on Epoch, 1985.
"Coming Round," Carolina Quarterly, vol. 37, no. 3 (Spring 1985) p. 34.
"Coming Clearing," "Anxiety's Prosody," "The Surprise of an Ending," and "All's All," Poetry, vol. CXLIII, no. 1 (October, 1988) pp. 1-4.
"Commentary," Poets and Writers, vol. 15, no. 2 (March/April 1987) p. 16.
"Country Music," Michigan Quarterly Review, vol. XVII, no. 4 (Fall 1978) p. 457.
"Day Ghosts," The New Yorker, April 15, 1985, p. 49.
"December Starlings," Beloit Poetry Journal, vol. 45, no. 4, p. 7.
"The Deep Slow," "Spot Check," and "Sparklings," Grand Street , vol. 7, no. 4 (Summer 1988) pp. 81-83.
"Distraction," "Design," "Rapids," "Feel like Traveling On," and "Spruce Woods," The Hudson Review, vol. XXXIII (Spring 1980) pp. 35-37.
"Easter Morning," Poetry, no. 1, vol. CXXXIV (April 1979) pp. 1-4.
"Enameling" and "Walking Around in the Evening," Cornell Review (Fall 1979) p. 74
"First Loves," Journal of the Poetry Society of America, no. 49 (Spring 1997) p. 9.
"Flat Rock," North Carolina Literary Review, no. 5, vol. II (1996) p. 70.
"For Emily Wilson from a Newcomer," Chelsea, no. 55, p. 13.
"Getting About," "Saving Account," and "Generally Clear with Scattered Slippery Spots," Panoply, no. 1, vol. 2 (Winter/Spring 1988) pp. 64-66. Includes note by Timothy Houghton.
"Gravelly Run," "Epiphany," and "Mansion," Poetry, no. 2, vol. XCVII (November 1960) pp. 94-97.
"The High Desiring,"The Grapevine's Finger Lakes Magazine (Winter 1986-87) p. 70.
"An Improvisation for the Pieties of Modernism," "An Improvisation for Angular Momentum," and "An Improvisation for Soot and Suet," Poetry, no. 1, vol. CLIX (October 1991) pp. 1-4.
"Inclinations," The Hudson Review, no. 1, vol. XL (Spring 1987) pp. 59-70.
"Keeping Track," North Carolina Literary Review, no. 1, vol. II (Spring 1994) p. 21.
"Laser," Foxfire, no. 1, vol. 1 (March 1967) p. 32. Includes note by Eliot Wigginton.
"Late Look," Mississippi Review, no. 7, vol. 3 (Fall 1978) p. 27.
"A Little Thing Like That," Bound, September 1986.
"Lofty Calling," "Obsession," "Weightlessness," and Walsh, William, "An Interview with A.R. Ammons," Michigan Quarterly Review, no. 1, vol. XXVIII (Winter 1989) pp. 105-120.
"Long Sorrowing," The New Yorker, October 20, 1986, p. 48.
"Middling Seasons," "Whitewater," and "Spike-Tooth Harrows," The Yale Review, vol. 80, no. 4 (October 1992) pp. 37-39.
"Modes Against Too Much," "Death and Silhouettes," "The Crystal Tree," The Paris Review, no. 127 (Summer 1993) pp. 34-37.
"Moving Figures," and "Prisons There and Not," Tar River Poetry, vol. 33 (Fall 1993) pp. 144-46.
"Museums," and "Time Being," Partisan Review, no. 1, vol. LVIII (Winter 1991) pp. 118-19.
"Narrows," Chicago Review. 26th Anniversary Poetry Issue, no. 3, vol. 26, p. 63.
"Now Then," Michigan Quarterly Review, no. 1, vol. XXXVI (Winter 1997) 185-187.
"Ontology Precedes Teleology," Skylark, XVII Issue, 1988-89, p. 7.
"Pairing" and "Course Discourse," Brim: An Arts Journal, no. 4 (Fall 1975) p. 18.
"A Part for the Whole," South-West Review, Spring 1987, p. 39.
"Period," Columbia: A Magazine of Poetry and Prose, no. 20 (Summer 1993) pp. 26-7.
"Picking Up Equations"The Paris Review, no. 134 (May 1978) p. 221.
"Ping Jockeys," Princeton University Library Chronicle, no. 3, vol. LV (Spring 1994) p. 468.
"A Pretty Looking Sight," Bluefish, no. 1, vol. 1 (Autumn 1983) p. 2.
"Radiant Days," CrossRoads: A Journal of Southern Culture, no. 1, vol. 1 (Fall 1992) pp. 45-48.
"Reading Ta'o Chien," The Southern Review, Autumn 1994, p. 791.
"Readings By Ways," Partisan Review, no. 4, vol. LV (1988) p. 611.
"The Sale Sale," "Blues in the Valley," "Turning Things Out Good," and "For My Beloved Son," The Hudson Review, no. 1, vol. XLVI (Spring 1993) p. 169-172.
"Saying Saying Away" and "The Clenched-Jaw School," Poetry, no. 3, vol. CLXVIII (June 1996) pp. 133-134.
"A Sense of Now," "Emerging Curves," "Local Antiquities," "Sumerian Vistas," "Collapsed Structures," and "The Deep End," Poetry, no. 1, vol. CLXVIII (April 1996) pp. 1-7.
"The Separation" and "Pit Lines," St. Andrews Review, no. 34 (Spring/Summer 1988) pp. 67-68.

"September Drift," "Mountain Talk," "Here & Now," "Square," "Admission," "Cougar," "Project," "Reversal," "Dominion," "Countering," "Working Still," "Then One," and "Play," Poetry, no. 1, vol. CXVII (October 1970) pp. 7-19.
"Singling and Doubling Together," "Meeting Place," "Yadkin Picnic," Poetry, no. 1, vol. CXLI (October 1982) pp. 1-4.
"Some Notes on William Harmon, 'Paronomastics,'" in Connotations, no. 1, vol. 3, 1993, pp. 42-43.
"Spring Tornado," "Certainty," "Model," and "Self-Projection," New Letters, no. 2, vol. 39 (Winter 1972) pp. 75-76.
"Standing Light Up," and "Plain Divisions," Verse, Issue 3, p. 3.
"Strings," Tar River Poetry, no. 1, vol. 28 (Fall 1988) pp. 60-61.
"From Strip," The Paris Review, no. 139 (Summer 1996) pp. 106-109.
"Summer Place," The Hudson Review, no. 2, vol. XXX (Summer 1977) pp. 173-209.
"Sung Reassertions," The Beloit Poetry Journal, (Fall 1963) p. 36.
"Taking Place" and "Contruing Deconstruction" Verse, no. 4, p. 8.
"Time After Time," "The Incomplete Life," and "What Was That Again," The Ohio Review, no. 41, 1988, 105-107.
"I Went Back," "Immortality," "Warming Trend" Beloit Poetry Journal, (Summer 1978) pp. 4-5.
"Weather-Bound," Poverty," "Density," "Sunday at McDonald's," "Vehicle," and "Response," The Hudson Review, no. 4, vol. XXXI (Winter 1978-79) pp. 587-590.

"Why Is It Always the Way It Always Is," "Motion Which Disestablishes Organizes Everything," "Forerunners," "The Time Rate of Change," "Loving People," "Ah," "Slights of Sight," and "Fall's End" The Hudson Review, no. 2, vol. XL (Summer 1987) pp. 286-294.
